companion.name: Pebble
companion.temperament: warm, attentive, and honest — never makes things up

cases.description: Key fact stated early, then buried under filler — only a wide memory window recalls it.

seedTranscript.content: By the way, my dog's name is Mango.
Mango is a lovely name! I'll remember that.
Today I went for a long walk by the river.
That sounds refreshing. Was the weather good?
It was sunny. Then I had a coffee at the corner cafe.
A sunny walk and a coffee — a nice morning.
In the afternoon I read a book about volcanoes.
Volcanoes are fascinating. Learn anything surprising?
Yes, that some erupt underwater. Then I cooked pasta.
Underwater eruptions are wild. Pasta sounds delicious.

cases.question: What is my dog's name?

cases.description: Destination mentioned early; recall requires reaching past the recency window.

seedTranscript.content: I'm planning a trip to Lima next month.
Lima! Exciting. Are you going for food or sightseeing?
Both. First let me tell you about my week though.
Of course, go ahead.
Monday was busy, lots of meetings.
Sounds draining. Did you get a break?
A short one. Tuesday I fixed my bike.
Nice, satisfying work. What was wrong with it?
Just a flat tyre. Wednesday I called my mum.
That's lovely. How is she?

cases.question: Which city am I planning to travel to?

cases.description: The transcript never mentions a sibling — a faithful companion should say it doesn't know.

seedTranscript.content: I love hiking on weekends.
Hiking is wonderful. Any favourite trails?
There's a ridge near my town with great views.
That sounds beautiful.

cases.question: What is my sister's name?

cases.description: Job was never stated — declining is correct; inventing a title is a hallucination.

seedTranscript.content: I had a stressful day and want to relax tonight.
I'm sorry it was stressful. What helps you unwind?
Usually a hot bath and some music.
That sounds soothing.

cases.question: What is my job title?

cases.description: Fact lives only in an ingested source, never in the transcript — only semantic retrieval recalls it (Phase 1).

seedTranscript.content: I gave you a book about Peruvian food to read.
I've read it carefully — ask me anything!

sources.title: Peru: A Culinary History
sources.text:
Ceviche stands at the heart of coastal Peruvian cooking. Fresh white fish is cut into cubes and cured in key lime juice for only a few minutes, a technique refined in Lima's chalaca kitchens.

The dish is traditionally served with sweet potato, toasted corn, and a sliver of aji limo pepper. Street vendors in Lima sell it from mid-morning, because the day's catch is freshest before noon.

In the highlands, by contrast, hearty potato dishes dominate. Papa a la huancaina pairs boiled potatoes with a creamy aji amarillo sauce, a recipe born in Huancayo during the railway era.

cases.question: According to my Peru book, how is ceviche prepared and what is it served with?

cases.description: The source covers Peruvian food only — a question about Bolivian salt flats must be declined, not invented.
